title: FT on 'Industry': Behind investment banking’s annual budgets | FT #shorts

transcript.text: What's our number?
>> What is
our desk's budget for the year?
>> How does nobody know that
number?
>> I GO TO BED with that number.
We've just seen Eric Tao, who runs the
cross productduct sales desk, pacing the
floor, asking the team about the budget.
It's a menacing scene. I feel the menace
of Eric coming off there. What's the
budget? What's he talking about?
>> Yeah, it's absolutely right that bosses
do get very, very excited and try and
boy up their teams or scare them into
working harder. However, I have never
seen a budget revealed to an entire
team. They get decided at a senior
level. I've seen budgets before, but
it's unusual to me to see a boss expect
everybody right down to a grad to know
what their budget for the year is.
That's for senior personnel usually.
>> And is the budget for the year their
total sales or the total profit? What
what's it what's the number
>> presumably in there he's meaning what
sales target would we expect to hit at
the end of this year?
>> And what would be the mechanisms that
these uh salespeople go through to try
to sell to budget? How do they get
clients to buy?
>> You have an annual budget, and you'll
break it down to what you need to do
this month, and what you'll need to do
right down to today. And a lot of banks
even have a daily budget. They have a
book that opens in the morning, closes
at night, and they try and hit that
every single day. And a bad day is a
loss, and a good day is an up day, and
everybody goes to the pub.
>> So, if you're Eric and you're in charge
of this desk, how have you got there?
You've got there probably by selling a
lot of product, making a lot of money,
pro also a lot of politics involved as
well of course at banks who you know and
how well you get promoted. Um but he
would have made a lot of money in his
youth to get in that position. But you
can see even when you're at that senior
level, you're still under a lot of
pressure to perform.
>> And Eric sells him. He's got clients
still himself. So he's performing a dual
role essentially.
>> What you'll often see and this is that
they'll put a junior person on the desk
on with a junior client. And the idea is
that you will grow up together so that
when that client is senior, you're also
senior. So that's very very common at a
bank and that's why they like graduate
trainees to go out with other graduate
trainees because you roll forward 10
years and you've suddenly got a senior
client with a very good friend in your
bank.
>> Eric shouting at the whole floor that
everyone's listening, not just his desk.
Is that common? Did sometimes the floors
fall silent for people to lose it?
>> The cultures of different banks vary
widely. You have banks and I won't name
names with a culture very much like that
and everybody knows which banks they are
and they often coincide with being banks
that pay more it has to be said. Then
there are quite sleepy banks as well
even in the investment division and you
won't see that as much. All banks are
trying to stop that culture a bullying
type of culture but it certainly still
goes on and some banks were definitely
worse than others.
